topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared glance across the crowded breakroom was electric, a sudden jolt of recognition that made my stomach clench. I was meticulously organizing my desk, every pen aligned with military precision, when Sarah walked in, humming a tune I vaguely remembered from last summer. Her smile, bright and unguarded, hit me like a physical blow. My fingers tightened around the stapler until my knuckles were white. "Hey, Mark," she chirped, oblivious to the internal tightening I was experiencing. "Long time no see!" My throat felt suddenly dry. I forced a smile, a stiff, practiced thing. "Sarah. Yeah, it has been." I avoided her eyes, focusing instead on the chipped paint on the coffee machine. Every movement felt deliberate, a careful navigation around this unexpected collision of shared history.

[story 2]

I kept checking my phone every few minutes, a habit I usually reserved for tracking project deadlines, but today it was different. The memory of that humid August, the smell of pine needles and campfire smoke—it was vivid, almost painful. When I saw Liam across the office floor, leaning against the water cooler, I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm tea. My breath hitched. I watched him, cataloging every subtle shift in his posture, searching for any clue that might confirm the impossible. He looked up, and for a split second, his eyes met mine. There was a flicker—a knowing, a deep-seated awareness that sent a cold tremor down my spine. I quickly looked away, my hands gripping the mug so hard the ceramic threatened to crack. I needed distance, needed to process this unexpected intrusion into my carefully constructed present.

[story 3]

It started with a misplaced photo on an old shared drive. I clicked on it, and there it was: a grainy picture of a muddy field, two identical tents, and a group of grinning faces—including Sarah’s. A wave of intense unease washed over me. I immediately shut the laptop, my heart hammering against my ribs. I started pacing the small space between my desk and the window, running a hand through my hair repeatedly. The silence of the office suddenly felt heavy, oppressive. I needed answers, needed to confirm this impossible connection. I glanced around the room, making sure no one was watching my sudden, agitated movements. The air felt thin, charged with an unspoken secret that had just been unearthed.
